Glossary About
Analytic Philosophy of Mind

Alan Turing

1912 – 1954

A developmental, computational functionalism: could machines think, and how would we know?

Alan Turing did not leave a systematic treatise in philosophy of mind. What he left is something more scattered but unusually fertile: a set of arguments, thought experiments, technical models, and research proposals that together amount to a distinctive philosophy of mind. The best reconstruction is that Turing develops a developmental, computational, anti-dualist, anti-solipsist functionalism. It is computational because he treats intelligent activity as something realizable by formal and mechanical processes; developmental because he thinks adult intelligence should be built through education, training, correction, and experience rather than by hand-coding a finished adult mind; anti-dualist because he rejects the appeal to souls or inaccessible mental essences as a barrier to machine intelligence; anti-solipsist because he treats the demand for first-person certainty about another mind as a destructive standard that would also undermine our belief in other human minds; and functionalist because he ties mental attribution to what a system can do, learn, say, remember, revise, and sustain across open-ended contexts.

The central point is easy to misunderstand. Turing is not simply saying, “If something behaves intelligently, it is conscious.” Nor is he saying, “Consciousness is unreal.” His view is subtler. He thinks that the question “Can machines think?” is badly posed if it demands a prior essence of “thinking,” so he replaces it with a public, experimental, language-mediated test. He then argues that the most common objections to machine intelligence—consciousness, souls, Gödelian creativity, emotion, originality, fallibility, and learning—do not establish any principled impossibility. In doing so, he brackets the deepest mystery of phenomenal consciousness without denying that it is a mystery. His position is therefore not eliminativist about consciousness; it is a methodological refusal to let the unsolved metaphysics of consciousness block the empirical and conceptual investigation of artificial intelligence. In the 1950 article, he explicitly frames the ordinary question as too dependent on undefined meanings of “machine” and “think,” and replaces it with the imitation game as a more tractable alternative.

1. The corpus: where Turing’s philosophy of mind appears

The relevant materials are mainly five clusters. First, Turing’s 1936 work on computability, which introduced the abstract machine model and the universal machine. That work is not a philosophy of consciousness, but it establishes the formal background for later claims: mechanical symbol manipulation can be described with exactness, and one universal machine can simulate other machines. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy notes that Turing’s formalism was intended to capture what a human being can do when following a procedure, and that the universal machine shows how programs can be treated as data.

Second, there is the 1948 report “Intelligent Machinery,” where Turing develops the ideas of “unorganized machines,” machine learning, neural-like networks, training, reward and punishment, and the relation between discipline and initiative. This paper is central because it shows that Turing’s philosophy of mind was not merely about a static verbal test. He was already thinking about artificial minds as systems that must be organized through developmental processes. In 1948 he discusses machines that can be modified by education, describes the cortex as possibly analogous to an initially unorganized machine, and distinguishes trained discipline from the additional “initiative” needed for intelligence.

Third, there is the 1950 paper “Computing Machinery and Intelligence,” the canonical source of the imitation game. This is the paper in which Turing most directly treats the objection from consciousness, the theological objection, the mathematical objection, Lovelace’s objection about originality, and the idea of child machines. He predicts that by the end of the twentieth century ordinary usage would make it possible to speak of machines thinking without contradiction, but he also says that the original question is too “meaningless” to answer directly in its unreconstructed form.

Fourth, there is the 1951 lecture “Intelligent Machinery, A Heretical Theory,” where Turing sharpens the claim that machines can do something very close to thinking, can make mistakes, can produce interesting new statements, can learn from experience, and may eventually outstrip human intelligence. This text is important for artificial consciousness because Turing there imagines machines with memory indexes, memories of past internal configurations, and even the ability to remember what their own thoughts had been.

Fifth, there is the 1952 BBC discussion “Can Automatic Calculating Machines Be Said To Think?” with M. H. A. Newman, Geoffrey Jefferson, and R. B. Braithwaite. The Turing Digital Archive identifies the broadcast as a BBC Third Programme discussion from January 1952, and the later published version describes it as one of the earliest recorded discussions of artificial intelligence. It also shows that Turing continued to refine the imitation game, including a version involving a jury rather than only a single interrogator.

2. Historical position: Turing between behaviorism, functionalism, computation, and artificial intelligence

Turing’s work appears at a decisive moment. Classical behaviorism had already pushed psychology away from private introspection and toward public evidence. Philosophical behaviorism and methodological behaviorism treated behavioral evidence as central to psychological explanation, partly because mental states seemed private and science demanded public criteria. Gilbert Ryle’s The Concept of Mind appeared in 1949, one year before Turing’s imitation-game paper, and Ryle was often read as replacing inner mental episodes with dispositions to behave, although that simple “behaviorist” label is itself contested.

Turing’s position overlaps with behaviorism but is not reducible to it. He shares the anti-Cartesian suspicion of private, occult mental essences as the sole basis for mental attribution. But unlike crude behaviorism, he is deeply interested in internal machinery: stored programs, memory, self-modification, random search, training histories, and internal decision procedures. He does not merely say that mind is behavior. He says that the relevant evidence for mind must be public, but the system that produces it may have rich internal organization.

Turing also precedes and anticipates functionalism. Later machine-state functionalism, associated especially with Hilary Putnam and later debates involving Block and Fodor, treats mental states as functional or machine-table states rather than as states defined by their biological substrate. The Stanford Encyclopedia’s entry on functionalism explicitly places this tradition in relation to Turing-machine models and probabilistic automata. Turing is therefore historically prior to the mature philosophical doctrine of functionalism, but many of functionalism’s core commitments are already present in him: substrate neutrality, role-based mental attribution, and the idea that mental capacities may be realized in many different physical systems.

Turing also precedes the mind-brain identity theory of the 1950s. Identity theorists such as Place, Feigl, and Smart argued that mental states are identical with brain states or processes, not merely correlated with them. Turing’s approach is very different. He does not require that a thinking system have human brain tissue. His view is more compatible with a broad physicalism than with type-identity theory: mental processes must be physically realized, but not necessarily in the same biological type of matter.

Finally, Turing stands at the beginning of later debates about artificial intelligence and artificial consciousness. Searle’s Chinese Room argument, introduced decades later, claims that a computer may manipulate symbols without understanding them and therefore that the Turing Test is inadequate as a test of genuine understanding. Turing does not answer Searle directly, of course, but his own writings already anticipate part of the problem: he worries about machines that are merely prearranged to pass a fixed test, and he emphasizes learning, correction, memory, and flexible experience rather than a static lookup table.

3. Turing’s methodological revolution: replacing essence with experiment

Turing’s most famous move is to refuse the ordinary question “Can machines think?” in its traditional form. He says that defining “machine” and “think” by ordinary usage would be dangerous, because it would make the question depend on shifting linguistic conventions. Instead, he proposes the imitation game: an interrogator communicates by text with hidden respondents and tries to determine which is which. Turing then asks what happens when a machine replaces one of the participants.

This shift has several philosophical implications.

First, Turing is not primarily offering a dictionary definition of thinking. He is replacing a metaphysically loaded question with a disciplined experimental question. The point is not that the word “thinking” has no meaning; the point is that disputes over its meaning can become sterile unless tied to publicly assessable capacities.

Second, the imitation game deliberately separates intellectual performance from irrelevant physical cues. Turing says the test draws a sharp line between physical and intellectual capacities: the interrogator does not see, touch, or hear the machine. This is not because Turing thinks embodiment is irrelevant to all intelligence; in other writings he discusses sensory organs, education, and human-like training. Rather, the test blocks prejudice based on appearance, voice, skin, sex, or visible mechanism.

Third, the test is dialogical. It is not a simple stimulus-response checklist. It asks whether a machine can sustain flexible, context-sensitive, linguistically mediated interaction with a human judge. That matters because Turing is not merely testing calculation. He is testing the ability to participate in the kind of open-ended exchange through which humans normally attribute intelligence to one another.

Fourth, Turing’s test is social and epistemic before it is metaphysical. It asks: under what conditions would we be justified in treating a machine as thinking? It does not fully answer: what is the intrinsic nature of consciousness? This is why later debates often misread Turing. The imitation game is not a full theory of phenomenal consciousness. It is a proposal about evidence, attribution, and the legitimacy of mental discourse about machines.

4. The argument from consciousness: Turing’s anti-solipsist response

The most important passage for consciousness is Turing’s reply to what he calls the “argument from consciousness.” He quotes Geoffrey Jefferson’s claim that a machine would not count as thinking unless it could produce something like a sonnet or concerto because of genuine thoughts and emotions, and unless it knew what it had produced. Jefferson’s objection is not merely about intelligence. It is about subjective experience: pleasure, grief, flattery, misery, charm, anger, depression, and self-awareness.

Turing’s reply is often caricatured. He does not say, “Consciousness is irrelevant.” He does not say, “Machines already feel.” He does not say, “Behavior logically entails experience.” Instead, he argues that Jefferson’s standard is too strong. If the only way to know whether a being thinks is to be that being, then we cannot know that any other human being thinks either. The demand for first-person access produces solipsism. Ordinary human life avoids solipsism by relying on public criteria: conversation, responsiveness, explanation, action, and the ordinary convention that other people think. Turing’s point is that machines should not be held to a uniquely impossible epistemic standard.

This is the core of Turing’s philosophy of consciousness. Consciousness is mysterious, but the mystery is not a license for asymmetric skepticism. If I accept behavioral, linguistic, and practical evidence for other human minds, then I need a principled reason to deny the same kind of evidence in the machine case. “It is made of silicon” or “it was manufactured” is not enough. The burden shifts to the critic: what exactly disqualifies the machine, if the same evidence would suffice for a human?

Turing also explicitly acknowledges that consciousness remains mysterious. He says he does not want to give the impression that there is no mystery about consciousness, but he denies that these mysteries must be solved before the machine-thinking question can be addressed. This is a crucial qualification. Turing is not solving the hard problem of consciousness in advance. He is saying that the metaphysical opacity of consciousness cannot be used selectively against machines unless one is prepared to become skeptical about other minds generally.

So Turing’s view is best described as anti-solipsist epistemic functionalism. Mental attribution is not based on direct access to another’s private experience. It is based on the role a system plays in a network of public, rational, linguistic, practical, and developmental relations. Consciousness is not denied, but third-person knowledge of it is mediated by evidence.

5. The theological objection: no privileged soul-barrier

Turing also treats the theological objection: the claim that thinking is a function of the immortal soul, and that God has given souls to humans but not to machines. Turing does not primarily answer by offering a full atheist metaphysics. Instead, he argues that the theological objector has no right to restrict divine freedom in that way. If one believes in divine omnipotence, it is not clear why God could not attach a soul to an elephant, or to a machine, if God wished. Human construction of a machine would not be the creation of a soul any more than human procreation is the creation of a soul.

The philosophical importance of this reply is that Turing refuses a category exclusion: the claim that machines are ruled out in principle because of their origin or material substrate. Even within the objector’s own theological framework, Turing says, the exclusion does not follow. In modern terms, this is an anti-chauvinist move. The mere fact that a system is artificial does not settle whether it can think or be conscious.

6. The mathematical objection: Gödel, limitation, and fallible minds

Turing also considers mathematical objections based on Gödelian incompleteness and related limitative results. The objection says, roughly, that formal machines are limited in ways that human mathematicians are not. Turing’s reply is cautious. He accepts that there are limitations to any particular formal system or machine, but he denies that this proves a human mind has no comparable limitations. It may show that any given machine can be defeated on some question, but it does not show that humans are unlimited or non-mechanical.

In the 1951 lecture, Turing also points out that mathematicians themselves make mistakes and that the discovery of new mathematical methods may change what problems are handled. He therefore resists the idealization of the human mathematician as an infallible oracle.

This is philosophically important because Turing does not need machines to be perfect. In fact, he repeatedly treats fallibility as a feature of intelligence rather than a disqualification. A machine that makes mistakes, revises itself, learns from correction, and produces surprising outputs is closer to human intelligence than a perfectly rigid calculator. This is one of the reasons Turing’s view cannot be reduced to the idea that intelligence is mere formal deduction. His artificial mind is not a theorem-proving statue; it is an educable, corrigible, historically shaped system.

7. Lovelace’s objection: originality, surprise, and machine creativity

Turing also addresses Ada Lovelace’s famous claim that Babbage’s Analytical Engine had no pretensions to originate anything, since it could only do what we know how to order it to perform. Turing’s answer depends on the universal machine and on the possibility that a machine’s behavior can surprise even its programmer. He grants that machines operate under rules, but he denies that this entails sterility. A machine may produce results not anticipated by its designers, especially when it is complex, trained, randomized, or self-modifying.

Turing’s reply is not merely “machines can output unexpected strings.” It is that the human sense of originality often depends on surprise, and machines can surprise us in relevant ways. Moreover, once we allow machines to learn, revise procedures, and search through possibilities, the boundary between “what the programmer put in” and “what the machine produced” becomes philosophically unstable. This anticipates later debates about creativity in artificial intelligence: whether creativity requires consciousness, intention, emotion, or whether novelty plus appropriate selection and value-sensitive production can suffice.

8. Computation and substrate-neutral mind

Turing’s computational philosophy of mind begins with his analysis of computation itself. His 1936 model did not arise from speculation about robots. It arose from an analysis of what a human “computer” does when carrying out a mechanical procedure. The formal machine captures rule-governed symbol manipulation step by step. Later computational theories of mind build on exactly this idea: if certain cognitive operations are formally specifiable, then they can in principle be realized by a suitable machine.

In the 1950 paper, Turing emphasizes the universality of digital computers: a digital computer can mimic any discrete-state machine, given enough storage and speed. This is crucial because it changes the philosophical question. We do not need a separate machine for every mental task. The deeper question becomes whether a sufficiently general machine can be programmed or trained into the relevant patterns of intelligent activity.

But Turing is not committed to the claim that the brain is literally a simple digital computer in every respect. In 1948 he discusses continuous and discrete machinery and suggests that the brain may be a continuous controlling machine, while also arguing that this does not create a decisive gulf between brains and artificial devices. He also considers electronic circuits in relation to nervous tissue and treats the problem as one of functional organization rather than biological essence.

This gives us one of the central theses of Turing’s philosophy of mind: the material substrate matters only insofar as it supports the relevant functional organization. Human neurons are one possible realization. Electronic circuits are another possible realization. What matters is not carbon versus metal, but whether the system can learn, reason, converse, remember, revise, and act intelligently.

9. Learning machines: Turing’s developmental theory of mind

The most underappreciated part of Turing’s philosophy is his theory of learning. The public image of Turing often reduces him to the imitation game, but his deeper research program is developmental.

In 1950, he asks why we should try to program an adult mind directly. Instead, he suggests building a child machine and educating it. He divides the adult mind into three factors: the initial state of the machine, the education to which it is subjected, and other experience. The child-machine proposal treats intelligence as the product of history, not merely architecture.

Turing compares this process to evolution. The child machine corresponds to hereditary material; modifications correspond to mutation; the experimenter’s judgment corresponds to selection. This is a strikingly modern view. It combines artificial intelligence, developmental psychology, evolutionary search, and machine learning into one conceptual program.

He also insists that reward and punishment need not presuppose felt pleasure or pain. In the 1950 paper, reward and punishment can function as training signals. They are part of an educational process, supplemented by other “unemotional” channels of communication. In 1948, he had already described “pleasure-pain systems” as trainable machines whose behavior could be shaped by external feedback.

This matters for artificial consciousness because Turing is not imagining a fully formed artificial subject appearing all at once. He is imagining an entity with a developmental trajectory. A machine becomes more mind-like as it is trained, corrected, exposed to experience, and reorganized. This is why Turing’s approach is closer to developmental functionalism than to simple behaviorism.

10. Black-box education and the opacity of artificial minds

Turing’s educational model also contains a striking anticipation of contemporary concerns about opacity. He says the teacher may be largely ignorant of what is going on inside the machine, just as human teachers do not know the detailed neural changes occurring in a pupil. The point is not that inner mechanisms are irrelevant; the point is that successful education does not require transparent access to every internal state.

In the 1951 lecture, Turing imagines a machine taught by a schoolmaster who communicates through language but lacks detailed knowledge of the machine’s inner workings. A mechanic may keep the machine running, but the educational process is distinct from mechanical maintenance.

This gives Turing’s philosophy a subtle epistemology. We can interact intelligently with systems whose inner mechanisms are not fully transparent. We do this with humans all the time. We know other minds through sustained, corrigible, socially embedded interaction, not through direct inspection of their neural or computational states. Turing extends that same pattern to machines.

11. Memory, self-reference, and proto-artificial consciousness

The 1951 lecture is especially important for artificial consciousness because Turing gives machines not merely behavior but memory, self-reference, and internal history. He imagines a machine whose memory contains indexes of experiences, including past configurations and thoughts, so that at a later stage it may begin to remember what its thoughts had been.

This is one of the closest points in Turing’s writings to a theory of artificial self-consciousness. He is not merely saying that a machine can store data. He is saying that it can store traces of its own prior cognitive configurations and use them in later decision-making. The machine can, in effect, have a history of itself available to itself.

He also imagines the machine investigating its own decision procedures. It may use present circumstances, past choices, and remembered outcomes to refine later choices. That is a primitive but real architecture for reflective agency: the system does not merely act; it can incorporate records of its own acts and internal states into future deliberation.

From this we can extract a Turingian account of artificial self-consciousness: a machine becomes a candidate for self-consciousness when it has stable memory of its own prior states, can refer to those states in reasoning, can modify itself in light of them, and can communicate about them in a way that survives questioning. Turing does not prove that such a machine has phenomenal experience. But he does identify many of the structural features that later theories associate with reflective consciousness.

12. Discipline and initiative: intelligence beyond rigid obedience

In the 1948 report, Turing distinguishes discipline from initiative. Converting a machine into a universal machine gives it extreme discipline: it can follow rules, execute procedures, and carry out formal transformations. But discipline alone is insufficient. Human intelligence also involves initiative—the capacity to search, vary, explore, propose, and depart from routine in fruitful ways.

This is essential to Turing’s view of mind. A mind is not just a rule-follower. It is a system in which rule-following, learning, search, correction, and controlled novelty are integrated. Turing’s repeated interest in random elements is part of this. He argues that randomness can be useful in learning because it enables search through possibilities. The point is not that randomness equals intelligence; it is that intelligence may require nontrivial exploration rather than deterministic repetition of a fixed routine.

This also helps answer the “mere simulation” worry. A machine that simply runs through a fixed script is not Turing’s ideal intelligent machine. Turing’s preferred machine has acquired capacities through education, can adapt to correction, can surprise, can make mistakes, can revise behavior, and can participate in an open-ended environment. This is why the imitation game should be read together with the child-machine and learning-machine proposals.

13. Intelligence as partly social and partly “emotional”

Turing also notices that our attribution of intelligence is itself psychologically and socially conditioned. In 1948 he discusses intelligence as, in part, an “emotional concept”: whether we are tempted to call something intelligent depends not only on the object but also on our own expectations, training, and ability to explain or predict its behavior. If a machine’s behavior becomes too predictable or too easily explained, people may withdraw the attribution of intelligence.

This is a subtle and important observation. Turing is not saying that intelligence is merely subjective. He is saying that intelligence attribution is partly relational. We recognize intelligence through interaction, surprise, difficulty, responsiveness, and social comparison. This explains why the imitation game is not just a laboratory trick. It dramatizes the social conditions under which humans are willing, or unwilling, to extend mental predicates.

It also anticipates a recurring pattern in AI history: once a task is mechanized, people often stop calling it intelligent. Chess, calculation, translation, and theorem proving may be treated as signs of intelligence before machines do them, and then downgraded after automation. Turing saw this tendency early.

14. Artificial consciousness: what Turing does and does not claim

The term “artificial consciousness” was not Turing’s standard term, and he does not provide a direct theory of phenomenal consciousness in machines. But his writings allow us to distinguish two questions.

The first is the attribution question: under what conditions are we justified in treating a machine as minded, intelligent, or conscious? Turing answers this through public, dialogical, developmental, and functional criteria. A machine that can converse, learn, remember, revise itself, produce novelty, explain itself, and sustain intelligent interaction over time becomes a serious candidate for mental attribution.

The second is the intrinsic phenomenal question: what would make there be something it is like to be that machine? Turing does not solve this. He explicitly admits that consciousness remains mysterious. His strategy is not to reduce phenomenal consciousness to behavior, but to deny that unresolved phenomenology gives us a principled reason to reject machine minds while accepting human minds on comparable evidence.

Therefore, Turing’s view of artificial consciousness is best reconstructed as follows: artificial consciousness is possible in principle if an artificial system realizes the relevant functional, linguistic, developmental, and self-referential organization; our evidence for it will be of the same general kind as our evidence for consciousness in other people; but the ultimate metaphysical nature of subjective experience remains an open problem.

15. A reconstructed Turingian philosophy of mind

Here is the most systematic extraction of Turing’s philosophy.

First, mental attribution is public but not superficial. We attribute minds through publicly available evidence: speech, action, learning, correction, memory, and responsiveness. But this does not mean mind is mere surface behavior. The surface is evidence of an underlying organized process.

Second, the other-minds problem applies equally to humans and machines. If one demands direct access to inner experience, one cannot know that other humans think. Since ordinary life rightly rejects solipsism, machines cannot be dismissed merely because their consciousness is not directly accessible.

Third, thinking is not tied in principle to biological tissue. The relevant question is whether the functional organization can be realized. Digital computers have the formal universality needed to simulate discrete-state machines, and Turing sees no principled reason why suitable machinery could not realize intelligent processes.

Fourth, minds are developmental achievements. Adult intelligence is not just an initial program. It is the result of initial structure plus education plus experience. This is one of Turing’s deepest insights: artificial minds should be grown, trained, and corrected, not merely assembled as finished adults.

Fifth, learning requires feedback, search, and modification. Reward, punishment, randomness, memory, and selection are not incidental. They are central to the formation of intelligent behavior. Turing’s learning machines are dynamic systems, not static calculators.

Sixth, intelligence includes fallibility. Machines need not be infallible to be intelligent. Human beings are not infallible either. Errors, correction, and improvement are marks of intelligent life, not disqualifications.

Seventh, originality is compatible with mechanism. A machine may produce results not foreseen by its designer, especially if it learns, searches, uses randomness, or modifies itself. The claim that a machine “only does what it is programmed to do” is too crude once programs can generate unanticipated consequences.

Eighth, self-consciousness requires memory of one’s own cognitive history. Turing’s 1951 account of machines remembering past configurations and thoughts points toward a functional account of self-consciousness: selfhood emerges when a system can record, retrieve, interpret, and use its own prior states.

Ninth, intelligence requires initiative as well as discipline. Pure rule-following is insufficient. A mind must be able to explore, initiate, revise, and search. Turing’s “discipline and initiative” distinction is one of his most philosophically rich contributions.

Tenth, the metaphysics of consciousness should not paralyze the science of mind. Turing admits that consciousness is mysterious, but he denies that the mystery must be solved before we investigate machine thinking. This gives his view a pragmatic structure: build, test, train, interact, and then refine the concepts.

16. Turing compared with later philosophy of mind

Against behaviorism, Turing accepts the need for public evidence but rejects the flattening of mind into mere behavior. His machines have internal organization, memory, learning rules, self-modification, and training histories. He is therefore better read as a precursor of functionalism than as a behaviorist.

Against identity theory, Turing denies that intelligence must be identified with human-brain types. He is compatible with physicalism, but not with a strict biological type-identity theory. Later identity theorists claimed that mental states are brain states; Turing’s framework instead suggests that the same mental role might be realized by different physical systems.

Against classical dualism, Turing rejects the idea that a hidden soul or private essence can be used to exclude machines from thought. His theological reply is especially revealing: even if one accepts a theological vocabulary, it does not follow that machines are metaphysically barred from thought or ensoulment.

Against later Chinese Room-style objections, Turing would likely insist on broadening the evidential situation. A static symbol manipulator in a room is not the full Turingian machine. The richer Turingian candidate is educated, historically embedded, corrigible, self-modifying, conversational, and capable of remembering its own past operations. That does not conclusively defeat Searle’s semantic objection, but it shows that Turing’s own view was more sophisticated than the caricature of a mere input-output test.

Against contemporary theories that make consciousness depend entirely on phenomenal feel, Turing’s answer is not a reduction but an epistemic challenge: whatever phenomenal consciousness is, our warrant for attributing it to others cannot depend on direct access. If machines eventually occupy the same evidential role as persons, denying them mentality will require more than an appeal to their artificial origin.

17. Critical limits of Turing’s position

Turing’s philosophy has real limits. The most important is that he does not give a theory of phenomenal consciousness. He gives a powerful argument about other minds and a research program for artificial intelligence, but he does not explain why or how functional organization gives rise to subjective experience. His own admission that consciousness remains mysterious is philosophically honest, but it leaves a gap.

A second limit is that the imitation game is anthropocentric. It tests whether a machine can perform in a human linguistic and cultural setting. That is useful if the question is whether machines can share human forms of intelligence, but it may exclude nonhuman forms of mind. Turing partially mitigates this by treating the imitation game as a replacement for a particular confused question, not as the sole possible measure of mentality.

A third limit is that language may not be sufficient for consciousness. A system might converse well without phenomenal awareness, as later critics argue. Turing’s strongest response is not that this is impossible, but that the same skeptical pressure applies to humans if pushed too far. That is a strong epistemic response, but not a complete metaphysical theory.

A fourth limit is that embodiment is underdeveloped in the canonical imitation game. Turing deliberately removes sight, touch, and voice from the test to avoid irrelevant prejudice. But embodied cognition theorists would later argue that perception, movement, affect, and bodily regulation are central to mind. Turing’s broader writings partly anticipate this concern because he discusses sensory organs, education, and even the possibility of imitating parts of the human body, but his most famous test remains heavily linguistic.

A fifth limit is ethical. Turing foresees that machine intelligence may eventually outstrip humans and even raises the possibility that machines could take control. But he does not develop a full moral philosophy of artificial persons. If machines could be conscious, then questions about rights, suffering, exploitation, responsibility, and political status would become unavoidable. Turing opens that door but does not walk through it.

18. Final synthesis: the philosophy of mind implicit in Turing

Turing’s philosophy of mind can be compressed into one formulation:

A system is minded to the extent that it can participate in the practices through which mindedness is recognized: it can learn from experience, respond intelligently in language, revise itself under correction, remember and use its own history, generate novelty, make and repair mistakes, act with initiative as well as discipline, and sustain these capacities across open-ended interaction. The system need not be biologically human. Its consciousness cannot be known by direct inspection, but neither can another human’s. Therefore, if its functional, developmental, and conversational life becomes sufficiently rich, refusing mental attribution solely because it is artificial becomes an instance of prejudice rather than philosophy.

That is the Turingian philosophy of artificial consciousness. It does not prove that any existing or future machine feels. It does something different: it dismantles the major a priori barriers to machine consciousness and proposes an empirical-developmental path by which artificial minds could become serious candidates for the same kind of mental attribution we extend to one another.

In the larger history of philosophy of mind, Turing stands between behaviorism and functionalism, between classical mechanism and modern AI, between the problem of other minds and the problem of artificial consciousness. His enduring contribution is not merely the “Turing Test.” It is the broader claim that mind should be understood through the organized capacities of systems in interaction: computation, learning, memory, self-reference, correction, language, and social recognition. He leaves the metaphysical mystery of consciousness unresolved, but he changes the burden of proof. After Turing, one cannot simply say, “It is a machine, therefore it cannot think.” One must explain why the same public, developmental, and rational evidence that supports belief in human minds would fail in the artificial case.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
